insights.title: Bank credit at ₹215.2L Cr — new all-time high
insights.body: Total bank credit outstanding reached ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, the highest level on record. Volume rose ₹3.0L Cr period-on-period (₹212.1L Cr → ₹215.2L Cr), reversing April's post-year-end dip and resuming the climb from ₹178.7L Cr in January 2025.
insights.implication: The credit cycle is back on track. Lenders should prepare for sustained high-volume deployment through FY27 — this is a growth environment, not a consolidation phase. Watch for capacity constraints and asset quality pressure as the system scales.

inferences: Bank credit hit ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, up ₹3.0L Cr from April's ₹212.1L Cr.
April's decline was a temporary correction; May's rebound confirms the underlying expansion trajectory remains intact.
The system has added ₹36.5L Cr since January 2025, with no sign of structural slowdown.

insights.title: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25, 17.1% in FY26 — 7.1pp step-up
insights.body: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration. The full-year growth rate jumped from 10.0% in the year ending March 2025 to 17.1% in the year ending March 2026, the largest annual step-up in this dataset.
insights.implication: The credit cycle has turned decisively. Banks and NBFCs that built capacity early are winning share; laggards face permanent disadvantage. Expect FY27 to sustain this pace unless macro headwinds emerge.

inferences: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration.
This is the sharpest year-over-year step-up recorded, signaling a decisive shift in the credit cycle.
Lenders that scaled underwriting and distribution in FY25 are now capturing compounding gains in FY26.

insights.title: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr in FY26 — record annual add
insights.body: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window. This figure has remained constant at ₹31.2L Cr across all nine periods, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
insights.implication: The system added more credit in one year than in any prior period tracked. Lenders must audit asset quality and capital adequacy headroom — growth at this scale demands heightened risk discipline.

inferences: Banks added ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the largest single-year deployment on record.
This volume exceeds any prior annual addition tracked, signaling aggressive system-wide expansion.
Growth at this velocity can mask emerging stress in weaker pockets or over-leveraged segments.

insights.title: Bank credit YoY at 17.7% — re-accelerating to record high
insights.body: Bank credit grew 17.7% year-on-year in May 2026, the highest growth rate in this dataset. Growth re-accelerated 1.6 percentage points (16.0% → 17.7%), reversing April's post-year-end slowdown and surpassing March's 17.1% peak.
insights.implication: April's slowdown was noise, not signal. Credit is expanding faster than at any point in this dataset. Lenders face intense competition for quality borrowers — expect pricing pressure, margin compression, and heightened underwriting discipline to sustain portfolio quality.

inferences: Bank credit grew 17.7% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 16.0% in April 2026.
April's deceleration was a temporary normalization; May's re-acceleration confirms the underlying momentum is intact.
Growth is now at the highest level recorded in this nine-period window, above the 16.5% upper range threshold.

insights.title: Food credit surged to ₹1.37L Cr — new all-time high
insights.body: Food credit outstanding jumped to ₹1.37L Cr in May 2026, the highest level on record. Volume rose ₹0.33L Cr period-on-period (₹1.04L Cr → ₹1.37L Cr), the second consecutive large increase and a new all-time high.
insights.implication: Food credit is a rounding error in the system, but the two-period surge signals active procurement financing. Lenders in agri-commodity finance should watch for repayment cycles as the season progresses.

inferences: Food credit hit ₹1.37L Cr in May 2026, up ₹0.33L Cr from April's ₹1.04L Cr.
This is the second straight period of sharp increases, following April's ₹0.34L Cr jump from March.
Food credit remains a small fraction of the ₹215L Cr system, but the surge signals active procurement financing.

insights.title: Food credit YoY at 94.1% — back near record high
insights.body: Food credit grew 94.1% year-on-year in May 2026, near the highest growth rate in this dataset. Growth re-accelerated 27.7 percentage points (66.4% → 94.1%), reversing April's sharp deceleration and returning to the upper range near February's 94.2% peak.
insights.implication: The triple-digit growth rates are compressing as the base normalizes, but food credit remains robust in absolute terms. Expect further rate volatility in coming periods as the low base from early 2025 rolls off.

inferences: Food credit grew 94.1% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 66.4% in April 2026.
April's deceleration was a temporary compression; May's re-acceleration confirms the underlying surge is intact.
Growth is now back near the 94.2% upper range threshold, driven by a low base from May 2025.

insights.title: Non-food credit at ₹213.8L Cr — new all-time high
insights.body: Non-food credit outstanding reached ₹213.8L Cr in May 2026, the highest level on record. Volume rose ₹2.7L Cr period-on-period (₹211.1L Cr → ₹213.8L Cr), reversing April's post-year-end dip and resuming the climb from ₹178.1L Cr in January 2025.
insights.implication: Non-food credit is where the real lending action is. The May rebound confirms April's dip was a post-year-end correction, not a trend reversal. Lenders should expect sustained high-volume deployment across agriculture, industry, services, and retail.

inferences: Non-food credit hit ₹213.8L Cr in May 2026, up ₹2.7L Cr from April's ₹211.1L Cr.
April's decline was a temporary correction; May's rebound confirms the underlying expansion trajectory remains intact.
Non-food credit drives the system-wide trend, accounting for 99.4% of total bank credit.

insights.title: Non-food credit added ₹30.8L Cr in FY26 — record annual deployment
insights.body: Banks deployed ₹30.8L Cr of net new non-food credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window. This figure has remained constant at ₹30.8L Cr across all nine periods, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
insights.implication: Non-food credit is where the real lending action is. The ₹30.8L Cr add signals aggressive deployment across productive sectors, but lenders must watch for pockets of over-leverage as growth sustains at this scale.

inferences: Banks added ₹30.8L Cr of net new non-food credit in FY26, the largest single-year deployment on record.
This volume accounts for 98.9% of the total ₹31.2L Cr system-wide addition, confirming non-food credit as the primary growth driver.
Growth at this scale demands heightened risk discipline to avoid pockets of over-leverage.

insights.title: Non-food credit YoY at 17.4% — re-accelerating to record high
insights.body: Non-food credit grew 17.4% year-on-year in May 2026, the highest growth rate in this dataset. Growth re-accelerated 1.5 percentage points (15.8% → 17.4%), reversing April's post-year-end slowdown and surpassing March's 16.9% peak.
insights.implication: April's slowdown was noise, not signal. Non-food credit is expanding faster than at any point in this dataset. Lenders should expect pricing pressure and margin compression in competitive segments as growth sustains above historical norms.

inferences: Non-food credit grew 17.4% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 15.8% in April 2026.
April's deceleration was a temporary normalization; May's re-acceleration confirms the underlying momentum is intact.
Growth is now at the highest level recorded in this nine-period window, above the 16.4% upper range threshold.

insights.title: All 4 main sectors growing — breadth at maximum
insights.body: All four main credit sectors — agriculture, industry, services, retail — posted positive year-on-year growth in May 2026. Breadth has held at 4 out of 4 sectors for the entire nine-period window, with no sector slipping into contraction.
insights.implication: Credit expansion is firing on all cylinders. Lenders face intense competition for quality borrowers in every sector — expect pricing pressure, margin compression, and heightened underwriting discipline to sustain portfolio quality.

inferences: All four main credit sectors posted positive year-on-year growth in May 2026, maintaining full breadth.
This is the ninth consecutive period with all sectors growing, the longest streak on record.
Broad-based expansion signals synchronized demand across agriculture, industry, services, and retail.

insights.title: Agriculture share steady at 12.36% — mid-range stability
insights.body: Agriculture's share of total bank credit stands at 12.36% in May 2026. Share declined 0.09pp from 12.44% last period, settling near the median of its nine-period range (12.3%–12.5%).
insights.implication: Agriculture maintains a stable 12.4% footprint — proportional growth signals balanced portfolio expansion. Lenders can sustain current allocation without rebalancing pressure; the sector is neither a share gainer nor a laggard.

inferences: Agriculture holds 12.36% of total credit, down 0.09pp from 12.44% last period.
The share has oscillated within a tight 12.3%–12.5% band for nine consecutive periods, with no sustained directional trend.
This stability indicates agriculture is growing in line with the overall credit pool, neither gaining nor losing structural ground.

insights.title: Agriculture credit at 14.91% YoY — re-accelerating after Apr dip
insights.body: Agriculture credit grew 14.91% YoY in May 2026, up from 13.70% last period. Growth re-accelerated 1.21pp from 13.70% to 14.91%, reversing the prior period's 1.92pp deceleration from the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62%. The trajectory shows: 13.7% → 14.9%.
insights.implication: Agriculture lending has regained momentum at 14.91% YoY after a brief Apr slowdown — the re-acceleration confirms the sector's strength is structural, not transient. Lenders should maintain or expand exposure; the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62% is within reach if the next period sustains this pace.

inferences: Agriculture credit grew 14.91% YoY in May 2026, up 1.21pp from 13.70% last period.
The prior period saw a 1.92pp deceleration from the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62%; this period reverses that move, signaling the Mar peak was not a one-time spike.
Growth is now above the upper range (p75 = 14.3%), confirming sustained momentum rather than a cooling trend.

insights.title: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25, 15.0% in FY26 — 6.8pp step-up
insights.body: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25 and 15.0% in FY26, marking a 6.8-point acceleration at the Mar 2026 year-end. The full-year acceleration of 6.8pp reflects a near-doubling of annual growth momentum from FY25 to FY26, the largest annual step-up in this dataset.
insights.implication: Industry credit has broken out of its multi-year slowdown — the 6.8pp acceleration confirms a capex-driven recovery is entrenched. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books, balance sheet repair, and PLI-linked demand. This is the most compelling industrial lending opportunity in years.

inferences: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25 and 15.0% in FY26, a 6.8pp acceleration measured at Mar 2026 year-end.
This near-doubling of annual growth confirms a capex-driven recovery has taken hold, breaking the sector's multi-year slowdown.
The acceleration is the largest in this dataset, signaling a structural shift in industrial credit demand.

insights.title: Industry share at 21.63% — edging up from nine-period low
insights.body: Industry accounts for 21.63% of total bank credit in May 2026. Share rose 0.13pp from 21.50% last period, moving off the nine-period low but remaining near the median of its range (21.5%–21.9%).
insights.implication: Industry is in a strong growth phase but not yet gaining share structurally — it is recovering, not reshaping the credit mix. Lenders can lean into industrial exposure for growth, but the sector is not displacing retail or services as the dominant driver. Watch for sustained share gains above 22% as a signal of structural rebalancing.

inferences: Industry holds 21.63% of total credit, up 0.13pp from 21.50% last period, which was the lowest level in this nine-period window.
Despite strong YoY growth (17.55%), the sector is not gaining share aggressively — it is recovering but not reshaping the credit mix.
The modest uptick suggests industry is growing slightly faster than the pool, but not enough to displace retail or services as dominant drivers.

insights.title: Industry credit at 17.55% YoY — new record, accelerating sharply
insights.body: Industry credit grew 17.55% YoY in May 2026, the fastest rate in this nine-period window. Growth accelerated 2.49pp from 15.06% last period, breaking the prior maximum of 15.62% and reaching a new high. The trajectory shows: 15.1% → 17.5%.
insights.implication: Industry lending has broken into new territory at 17.55% YoY — the sharpest acceleration in this window confirms the capex-driven recovery is intensifying. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books, infrastructure exposure, and balance sheet repair. Watch for any deceleration below 15% as a signal of peak momentum passing.

inferences: Industry credit grew 17.55% YoY in May 2026, up 2.49pp from 15.06% last period and surpassing the prior peak of 15.62%.
The acceleration is the sharpest single-period jump in this window, confirming the capex-driven recovery is intensifying, not plateauing.
Growth is now at or near the maximum in this dataset, signaling peak momentum in industrial lending.

insights.title: Personal loans at 32.81% — slipping from Apr peak
insights.body: Personal loans account for 32.81% of total bank credit in May 2026. Share declined 0.16pp from 32.98% last period, slipping from above the 75th percentile (32.9%) back toward the median of its range (32.7%–32.9%).
insights.implication: Personal loans have given back half of last period's share gain, settling at 32.81% — the Apr bounce was temporary, not a sustained shift back to retail-heavy portfolios. The sector is growing in line with the pool, not reshaping the mix. Lenders should maintain current exposure but avoid over-indexing to retail; the rebalancing toward productive sectors remains intact.

inferences: Personal loans hold 32.81% of total credit, down 0.16pp from 32.98% last period, which was near the top of the nine-period range.
The prior period saw a 0.38pp jump from 32.59% to 32.98%; this period reverses half that move, suggesting the Apr bounce was temporary.
The share is now near the median, indicating personal loans are growing in line with the pool, not gaining structural ground.

insights.title: Personal loans at 15.38% YoY — decelerating from Apr high
insights.body: Personal loans credit grew 15.38% YoY in May 2026, down from 16.01% last period. Growth decelerated 0.64pp from 16.01% to 15.38%, slipping from near the upper range (p75 = 15.7%) toward the median. The trajectory shows: 16.0% → 15.4%.
insights.implication: Personal loans remain a growth engine at 15.38% YoY, but the deceleration from 16.01% signals the sector may be plateauing after a strong run. Lenders should monitor unsecured exposure — especially credit cards and personal loans — as growth stabilizes at elevated levels. Watch for further softening below 14% as a signal of peak momentum passing.

inferences: Personal loans grew 15.38% YoY in May 2026, down 0.64pp from 16.01% last period.
The prior period was near the highest rate in this window; this period marks a pullback toward the median (p25 = 11.8%, p75 = 15.7%).
The deceleration suggests the sector may be plateauing after a strong run, with growth stabilizing in the mid-teens.

insights.title: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25, 19.0% in FY26 — 7.0pp step-up
insights.body: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, marking a 7.0-point acceleration at the Mar 2026 year-end. The full-year acceleration of 7.0pp is the sharpest annual step-up in this dataset, reflecting a near-doubling of annual growth momentum from FY25 to FY26.
insights.implication: Services credit is in its strongest cycle in recent years — the 7.0pp acceleration confirms a structural shift, not a temporary spike. Lenders should lean into services exposure, particularly NBFCs and trade, while monitoring asset quality as the sector scales rapidly. This is the most compelling services lending opportunity in this dataset.

inferences: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, a 7.0pp acceleration measured at Mar 2026 year-end.
This near-doubling of annual growth confirms a structural shift in services credit demand, not a temporary spike.
The 7.0pp acceleration is the largest in this dataset, signaling services is in its strongest cycle in recent years.

insights.title: Services share at 28.28% — edging up from Apr dip
insights.body: Services account for 28.28% of total bank credit in May 2026. Share rose 0.07pp from 28.21% last period, recovering slightly from the prior period's 0.26pp decline but remaining below the Mar 2026 peak of 28.47%. The trajectory shows: 28.2% → 28.3%.
insights.implication: Services remain a dominant force in the credit mix at 28.28%, holding near the top of their range despite the Apr dip. The sector is still capturing a larger slice of the pool than a year ago. Lenders should maintain services exposure but watch for signs of saturation as share stabilizes in the 28.2%–28.5% band.

inferences: Services hold 28.28% of total credit, up 0.07pp from 28.21% last period, which marked a pullback from the Mar 2026 peak of 28.47%.
The share remains above the upper range (p75 = 28.2%), indicating services are still capturing a larger slice of the pool than a year ago.
The modest uptick suggests services are stabilizing near the top of their range, not losing structural ground despite the Apr dip.

insights.title: Services credit at 20.4% YoY — highest on record, strengthening
insights.body: Services credit grew 20.4% year-on-year in May 2026, the highest reading in this 9-period dataset. Growth accelerated from 18.6% in April 2026 to 20.4% in May 2026, a 1.82-point jump. The trajectory over the past year shows: 12.0% → 15.5% → 16.3% → 19.0% → 18.6% → 20.4%.
insights.implication: Services credit has broken through the March peak and is now at its fastest growth rate on record. The April deceleration was a false signal — momentum has resumed. Lenders should lean into services exposure: the strengthening status and record 20.4% growth confirm this is the sector to prioritise. If growth holds above 20% in the next period, the acceleration phase is entrenched.

inferences: Services credit grew 20.4% year-on-year, the highest rate in this dataset and a 1.82-point acceleration from the prior period's 18.6%.
The prior period's 0.42-point deceleration (19.0% → 18.6%) has reversed — growth has re-accelerated past the March peak.
This confirms the March high was not a ceiling but a pause in a sustained uptrend.

insights.title: Large corporates surged 14.4% YoY — highest in 9 periods
insights.body: Large corporate credit grew 14.4% YoY in May 2026, the strongest reading in this nine-period window. Growth accelerated from 10.0% last period to 14.4%, extending a recovery that began at 5.5% in Jan 2026. The 4.34pp jump is the sharpest single-period acceleration on record.
insights.implication: Large corporates are re-accelerating sharply — the 14.4% pace is the highest in nine periods and confirms the segment is back in growth mode. However, the 14.4% pace is insufficient to defend market share against 26.2% Micro & Small growth and 21.2% Medium growth. Lenders here are capturing healthy flow, but the incremental credit opportunity is overwhelmingly in MSME segments.

inferences: Large corporate credit grew 14.4% YoY, up from 10.0% last period — a 4.34pp acceleration, the sharpest in this dataset.
The segment has re-accelerated from 5.5% in Jan 2026 to 14.4% in May 2026, signaling a structural recovery.
Despite the 14.4% pace, large corporates are losing share to MSME segments growing above 20% — the growth is healthy but structurally outpaced.

insights.title: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% share — stable for 5 periods
insights.body: Medium enterprises account for 9.7% of industry credit in May 2026, down 0.05pp from the prior period. Share rose from 8.9% in Jan 2025 to 9.7% by Jan 2026 and has held at 9.7% for five consecutive periods, with the 0.05pp dip this period being noise.
insights.implication: Medium enterprises have carved out a 9.7% share and are holding it. The five-period plateau signals the segment is growing in line with the broader industry pool, not outpacing it. Lenders here are capturing steady flow, not outsized growth — the incremental opportunity is in Micro & Small, not Medium.

inferences: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% of industry credit, unchanged from 9.7% in Apr 2026 (the 0.05pp dip is rounding noise).
The segment has held 9.7% share for five straight periods, signaling it is growing in line with the broader industry pool.
The five-period plateau confirms Medium enterprises are capturing steady flow, not outsized growth.

insights.title: Medium enterprises grew 21.2% YoY — re-accelerating
insights.body: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.2% YoY in May 2026, up 1.29pp from the prior period. Growth re-accelerated from 19.9% last period to 21.2%, after decelerating from a peak of 22.3% in Jan 2026. The 1.29pp uptick reverses the prior two-period slowdown.
insights.implication: Medium enterprises are re-accelerating after a two-period slowdown. The 21.2% pace is healthy and far ahead of large corporates at 14.4%, confirming the segment remains a strong outperformer. Lenders should watch for sustained acceleration above 21% — if growth holds here, Medium enterprises will resume share gains.

inferences: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.2% YoY, up from 19.9% last period — a 1.29pp re-acceleration.
The segment had decelerated from 22.3% in Jan 2026 to 19.9% in Apr 2026; the 21.2% reading reverses that trend.
The 21.2% pace is far ahead of large corporates at 14.4%, confirming Medium enterprises remain a strong outperformer.

insights.title: Micro & Small grew 32.7% in FY26 vs 8.9% in FY25 — 23.8pp step-up
insights.body: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 32.7% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 23.8 percentage point acceleration. Full-year growth jumped from 8.9% in FY25 to 32.7% in FY26 — the sharpest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
insights.implication: This is the defining inflection in industry credit. Micro & Small lending has entered a new growth regime, and lenders with exposure here are riding the fastest-growing segment. The 23.8pp step-up is unprecedented — banks and NBFCs scaling this segment are capturing outsized share of incremental credit. The FY26 rate of 32.7% is 3.7× the FY25 rate — this is structural, not cyclical.

inferences: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in FY25 and 32.7% in FY26 — a 23.8pp step-up, the largest in this dataset.
The 23.8pp acceleration confirms Micro & Small lending has entered a new growth regime, not a cyclical uptick.
Lenders with exposure here are riding the fastest-growing segment in industry credit — the step-up is unprecedented.

insights.title: Micro & Small hold 23.1% share — up 3.1pp in 12 months
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprises account for 23.1% of industry credit in May 2026, down 0.06pp from the prior period. Share surged from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.5% in Jan 2026, then eased to 23.1% — a net gain of 3.1 percentage points in 14 months. The 0.06pp dip this period is noise against the broader structural gain.
insights.implication: Micro & Small borrowers are capturing share at the expense of large corporates. The 3.1pp gain in 14 months is a structural reallocation, not noise. Lenders focused on large corporates are being left behind — the incremental credit opportunity is overwhelmingly in smaller borrowers. The 0.06pp dip this period is noise, not a reversal.

inferences: Micro & Small enterprises hold 23.1% of industry credit, down 0.06pp from 23.1% last period (rounding noise).
The segment gained 3.1pp share from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.1% in May 2026 — the sharpest structural shift in this dataset.
The 3.1pp gain is a direct transfer from large corporates, which lost 3.7pp over the same window.

insights.title: Micro & Small grew 26.2% YoY — decelerating but still dominant
insights.body: Micro & Small credit grew 26.2% YoY in May 2026, down 3.91pp from the prior period. Growth decelerated from 30.1% last period to 26.2%, after peaking at 32.7% in Mar 2026. The segment has now decelerated for two consecutive periods, losing 6.5pp from the peak.
insights.implication: Micro & Small credit is decelerating but remains the hottest segment in industry credit. The 26.2% pace is still 1.8× the large corporate rate of 14.4%, confirming the segment is capturing the bulk of incremental credit. Lenders should watch for further deceleration — if growth drops below 25%, the segment risks losing share. The two-period slowdown is the first sign of momentum loss after four periods above 30%.

inferences: Micro & Small credit grew 26.2% YoY, down from 30.1% last period — a 3.91pp deceleration.
The segment has decelerated for two straight periods, losing 6.5pp from the 32.7% peak in Mar 2026.
Despite the slowdown, the 26.2% pace is still 1.8× the large corporate rate of 14.4% — the segment remains the dominant growth driver.

insights.title: MSME outpacing large corporates by 11.8pp — spread narrowing sharply
insights.body: MSME credit is growing 11.8 percentage points faster than large corporate credit in May 2026. The spread narrowed from 20.1pp last period to 11.8pp, after widening from 2.1pp in Mar 2025 to 25.7pp in Jan 2026. The 8.3pp collapse this period is the sharpest single-period narrowing on record.
insights.implication: The growth divergence between MSME and large corporates is narrowing sharply — large corporates are re-accelerating while MSME decelerates. However, the 11.8pp spread confirms MSME segments are still capturing the bulk of incremental credit. Lenders overweight to large corporates are seeing improved flow, but the incremental opportunity remains in MSME. Watch for further convergence — if the spread drops below 10pp, large corporates will start defending share.

inferences: The MSME vs large corporate growth spread narrowed from 20.1pp to 11.8pp — an 8.3pp collapse, the sharpest in this dataset.
Large corporates re-accelerated from 10.0% to 14.4% while MSME decelerated from 30.1% to 26.2%, driving the convergence.
Despite the narrowing, the 11.8pp spread confirms MSME segments are still capturing the bulk of incremental credit.

gaps.title: Large corporates hold 67.3% share — down 3.8pp in 6 periods
gaps.body: Large corporates account for 67.3% of industry credit in May 2026, up 0.11pp from the prior period. Share compressed from 71.0% in Jan 2025 to 67.3% in May 2026, losing 3.7 percentage points over six periods. The 0.11pp uptick this period is noise against the broader structural decline.
gaps.implication: Large corporates are losing their grip on industry credit. The 3.7pp share loss in six periods is the sharpest structural shift in this dataset. Lenders overweight to large corporates are missing the growth — incremental credit is flowing to MSME borrowers. The 0.11pp uptick this period is noise, not a reversal.

inferences: Large corporates hold 67.3% of industry credit, down from 71.0% in Jan 2025 — a 3.7pp loss in six periods.
The 0.11pp uptick this period is the first gain in four periods, but it is too small to signal a trend reversal.
MSME segments are capturing incremental credit at the expense of large corporates — the share loss is structural, not cyclical.

insights.title: Housing Finance Companies holds 17.7% of NBFC credit; Public Financial Institutions 17.0%
insights.body: RBI breaks NBFC credit into two sub-categories. Housing Finance Companies holds 17.7% and Public Financial Institutions 17.0% — these are size shares, not growth rates. Together the two break-outs cover 34.7% of NBFC credit; the rest is not sub-classified in the RBI statement.
insights.implication: Read this as size mix, not momentum: the two blocks are close in size — a move in either shifts NBFC credit. Check the matching growth scan before positioning.

inferences: Housing Finance Companies is the larger break-out at 17.7% of NBFC credit.
Public Financial Institutions holds 17.0% — a 0.7 percentage points gap between the only two break-outs.
Together they cover 34.7% of NBFC credit; RBI does not sub-classify the rest.

insights.title: Public Financial Institutions growing at 71.8% YoY; Housing Finance Companies at 19.7%
insights.body: Public Financial Institutions grew 71.8% year-on-year against Housing Finance Companies at 19.7% — a 52.0 percentage points growth gap between the two sub-categories RBI breaks out.
insights.implication: Lenders can lean into Public Financial Institutions (71.8%) and monitor Housing Finance Companies (19.7%). Broad participation supports diversified deployment.

inferences: Public Financial Institutions is the faster of the two at 71.8% YoY.
Housing Finance Companies is at 19.7% — 52.0 percentage points behind.
Both are growing — the gap is about pace, not direction.

insights.title: Non-Banking Financial Companies is the biggest slice of services credit at 34.5%
insights.body: Non-Banking Financial Companies holds the largest share of services credit at 34.5%, Trade 22.5%; Shipping is the smallest at 0.2%. These are size shares, not growth rates. Top three hold 77.3% of services credit, spread 34.4 percentage points.
insights.implication: Composition, not momentum: Non-Banking Financial Companies carries the most weight of services credit. Concentration means the big blocks drive the category — watch them first.

inferences: Non-Banking Financial Companies is the largest block at 34.5% of services credit.
Shipping is the smallest at 0.2% — a 34.4 percentage points spread across 10 sub-categories.
The top three hold 77.3% of services credit — concentrated mix.

insights.title: Computer Software growing fastest at 54.9%; Transport Operators slowest at 7.6%
insights.body: Computer Software leads YoY growth at 54.9%, Shipping at 41.0%; Transport Operators is slowest at 7.6%. 10 of 10 categories growing, spread 47.3 percentage points.
insights.implication: Lenders can lean into Computer Software (54.9%) and monitor Transport Operators (7.6%). Broad participation supports diversified deployment.

inferences: Computer Software is the standout at 54.9% YoY.
Transport Operators is the weakest at 7.6% — a spread of 47.3 percentage points across 10 categories.
10 of 10 categories are growing — broad-based momentum.

insights.title: Wholesale Trade holds 56.4% of trade credit; Retail Trade 43.6%
insights.body: RBI breaks trade credit into two sub-categories. Wholesale Trade holds 56.4% and Retail Trade 43.6% — these are size shares, not growth rates. Together the two break-outs cover 100.0% of trade credit — the full category.
insights.implication: Read this as size mix, not momentum: the two blocks are close in size — a move in either shifts trade credit. Check the matching growth scan before positioning.

inferences: Wholesale Trade is the larger break-out at 56.4% of trade credit.
Retail Trade holds 43.6% — a 12.8 percentage points gap between the only two break-outs.
Together they cover 100.0% of trade credit — the full category.

insights.title: Wholesale Trade growing at 22.3% YoY; Retail Trade at 11.3%
insights.body: Wholesale Trade grew 22.3% year-on-year against Retail Trade at 11.3% — a 10.9 percentage points growth gap between the two sub-categories RBI breaks out.
insights.implication: Lenders can lean into Wholesale Trade (22.3%) and monitor Retail Trade (11.3%). Broad participation supports diversified deployment.

inferences: Wholesale Trade is the faster of the two at 22.3% YoY.
Retail Trade is at 11.3% — 10.9 percentage points behind.
Both are growing — the gap is about pace, not direction.

insights.title: Credit card outstanding at ₹2.95L Cr — down ₹3,481 Cr
insights.body: Credit card outstanding fell to ₹2.95L Cr in May 2026, down ₹3,481 Cr from ₹2.98L Cr in Apr 2026. Volume declined ₹2.98L Cr → ₹2.95L Cr, the second consecutive monthly drop after peaking in Apr 2026.
insights.implication: The Apr 2026 uptick was a false dawn — card lending is contracting again. Lenders should treat this as confirmation that the low-growth regime is entrenched, not transitional. Focus on portfolio quality and yield, not volume recovery.

inferences: Credit card outstanding fell ₹3,481 Cr to ₹2.95L Cr in May 2026, reversing the prior month's gain.
This is the second consecutive decline after the Apr 2026 peak of ₹2.98L Cr.
The sequential contraction signals renewed weakness in card lending despite the earlier stabilisation narrative.

insights.title: Credit cards below 10% YoY for 7 straight periods — record run
insights.body: Credit card growth has remained below 10% YoY for 7 consecutive periods, the longest such run in this dataset. The sub-10% streak extended from 6 periods in Apr 2026 to 7 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.
insights.implication: Seven months of sub-10% growth is not a cycle — it is the new baseline. Card lenders must permanently reset volume expectations and shift strategy toward margin optimisation and risk-adjusted returns. No recovery is visible.

inferences: Credit card growth has stayed below 10% YoY for 7 consecutive periods, the longest run on record.
The streak began in Dec 2025 and has now extended through May 2026 without interruption.
This confirms a structural shift away from the double-digit growth regime that prevailed before Dec 2025.

insights.title: Credit card share at 4.20% — lowest in 9 periods
insights.body: Credit cards now account for 4.20% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window. Share declined from 4.28% in Apr 2026 to 4.20% in May 2026, continuing the downtrend from 5.0% in Jan 2025.
insights.implication: Cards are no longer a growth driver for retail lenders — they are losing share in a growing pool. This is a structural shift toward secured lending that will persist. Lenders should reallocate capital to gold and housing.

inferences: Credit card share fell to 4.20% in May 2026, the lowest reading in 9 periods.
The 0.8-point decline from 5.0% in Jan 2025 reflects sustained underperformance versus other retail segments.
Gold and housing are capturing the incremental credit flows, leaving cards structurally marginalised.

insights.title: Credit card growth at 1.33% YoY — down 2.5 points from Apr
insights.body: Credit card outstanding grew 1.33% YoY in May 2026, down from 3.78% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 3.78% → 1.33%, erasing the prior month's uptick and returning to the Dec 2025–Feb 2026 range of 1.0%–1.7%.
insights.implication: The Apr 2026 uptick was noise, not signal — card lending remains structurally subdued at 1.3% YoY. Lenders should abandon volume-growth targets and focus on yield optimisation and portfolio quality through H1 FY27.

inferences: Credit card growth fell to 1.33% YoY in May 2026, down 2.45 points from 3.78% in Apr 2026.
The Apr 2026 uptick to 3.8% has reversed — growth is back near the 1.0%–1.7% range seen in Dec 2025–Feb 2026.
This confirms the Apr 2026 reading was an outlier, not the start of a recovery.

insights.title: Consumer Durables share at 0.33% — near irrelevance
insights.body: Consumer Durables now represent 0.33% of personal loans, near the median of the 9-period range. Share edged up from 0.32% in Apr 2026 to 0.33% in May 2026, but remains down from 0.4% in Jan 2025.
insights.implication: Consumer Durables are no longer a meaningful retail lending category — they represent one-third of one percent of the pool. Lenders have effectively exited. No meaningful origination or portfolio growth expected.

inferences: Consumer Durables account for just 0.33% of personal loans in May 2026.
The 0.1-point decline from 0.4% in Jan 2025 reflects sustained underperformance and lender exit.
This segment is shrinking into irrelevance within retail credit.

insights.title: Education loans at 2.25% share — lowest in 9 periods
insights.body: Education loans account for 2.25% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window. Share edged up from 2.24% in Apr 2026 to 2.25% in May 2026, but remains down from 2.3% in Jan 2025.
insights.implication: Education lending is growing but losing share in a growing pool — it is not a growth engine. This is a stable, low-volatility portfolio suitable for defensive capital allocation, not volume expansion.

inferences: Education loans represent 2.25% of personal loans in May 2026, the lowest share in 9 periods.
The 0.1-point decline from 2.3% in Jan 2025 reflects slower growth versus gold and housing.
Education lending is growing but losing ground relative to faster-moving segments.

insights.title: Education loans at 14.03% YoY — re-accelerating after 6-period slide
insights.body: Education loans grew 14.03% YoY in May 2026, up from 13.27% in Apr 2026. Growth re-accelerated 13.27% → 14.03%, reversing a 6-period deceleration from 15.9% in Jan 2025 to 13.3% in Apr 2026.
insights.implication: Education lending has stabilised at 14% YoY after a 6-period deceleration. This is a defensive, low-risk segment — expect continued mid-teens growth but no acceleration. Suitable for stable, risk-adjusted returns.

inferences: Education loans grew 14.03% YoY in May 2026, up 0.76 points from 13.27% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration in 7 periods, reversing a 2.6-point slide from 15.9% in Jan 2025.
The uptick suggests the deceleration has bottomed, though growth remains below the 15%+ rates seen a year ago.

insights.title: FD-backed advances at 2.21% share — lowest in 9 periods
insights.body: Advances against Fixed Deposits now represent 2.21% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window. Share declined from 2.27% in Apr 2026 to 2.21% in May 2026, continuing the retreat from the 2.5% peak in Dec 2025.
insights.implication: The defensive shift toward FD-backed lending peaked in Dec 2025 and is now unwinding. Lenders are still prioritising this zero-risk product, but the structural pivot has stabilised. Expect share to hold near 2.2%.

inferences: FD-backed advances account for 2.21% of personal loans in May 2026, the lowest share in 9 periods.
The 0.3-point decline from 2.5% in Dec 2025 reflects normalisation after the defensive surge.
The structural pivot toward zero-risk FD-backed lending is moderating.

insights.title: FD-backed advances at 8.06% YoY — down 1.6 points, lowest in 9 periods
insights.body: Advances against Fixed Deposits grew 8.06% YoY in May 2026, down from 9.61% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 9.61% → 8.06%, the lowest reading in this 9-period window.
insights.implication: The Mar 2026 spike to 19.2% was a one-time event, not a sustained acceleration. FD-backed lending is decelerating to single digits — the defensive pivot has ended. Expect mid-single-digit growth ahead, not the double-digit rates seen earlier.

inferences: FD-backed advances grew 8.06% YoY in May 2026, down 1.56 points from 9.61% in Apr 2026.
This is the lowest growth rate in 9 periods, confirming the Mar 2026 spike to 19.2% was a one-time event.
The deceleration from 19.2% to 8.1% over two months confirms the defensive surge has ended.

insights.title: Gold loans 12 periods positive — longest streak on record
insights.body: Gold loans have posted positive YoY growth for 12 consecutive periods, the longest unbroken expansion streak in this dataset. The positive streak extended from 11 periods in Apr 2026 to 12 periods in May 2026, reaching a new historical maximum.
insights.implication: Gold lending is the most reliable growth engine in retail credit. The 12-period streak confirms entrenched, structural momentum — lenders should expand gold loan capacity aggressively and treat this as a multi-year portfolio pillar.

inferences: Gold loans have grown every period for 12 months straight — the longest run on record.
This is the most consistent growth story in retail credit, with no interruption since the streak began.
The unbroken momentum confirms this is a structural shift, not a cyclical bounce.

insights.title: Gold loans hit 7.33% share — highest on record
insights.body: Gold loans now account for 7.33% of personal loans, the highest share in this 9-period window. Share surged from 3.0% in Jan 2025 to 7.3% in May 2026 — more than doubling in 16 months.
insights.implication: Gold lending has become a structural pillar of retail credit, capturing incremental flows faster than any other segment. This is the single biggest portfolio shift in personal loans — lenders should expand gold loan capacity aggressively to capture this momentum before it plateaus.

inferences: Gold loans' share jumped from 7.0% in Apr 2026 to 7.3% in May 2026, a 0.31pp gain in one period.
This extends the year-long climb from 3.0% to 7.3% — a 4.3pp structural shift.
Gold is displacing other personal loan categories at an accelerating rate.

insights.title: Gold loans 105.5% YoY — still fastest-growing segment
insights.body: Gold loans grew 105.5% YoY in May 2026, down from 121.2% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated by 15.7 points period-on-period (121.2% → 105.5%), the sharpest single-period slowdown in this window.
insights.implication: Gold lending remains the fastest-growing personal loan category by a wide margin. The sharp deceleration suggests the explosive phase is plateauing, but the 105% pace is still structural, not cyclical. Lenders with gold loan capacity should continue to lean in — this is a multi-year shift, and the current rate still justifies aggressive expansion.

inferences: Gold loans grew 105.5% YoY in May 2026, down from 121.2% in Apr 2026 — a 15.7-point deceleration.
This is the steepest one-period slowdown in the series, but the 105.5% pace still far outstrips all other personal loan categories.
The explosive phase is cooling, but the growth rate remains more than double the next-fastest segment.

insights.title: Housing 10.9% YoY — weakest growth in 9 periods
insights.body: Housing loans grew 10.9% YoY in May 2026, down from 11.4% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated by 0.5 points period-on-period (11.4% → 10.9%), falling to the lower end of the 9-period range (10.7% to 11.5%).
insights.implication: Housing demand is stable but decelerating, and the 10.9% pace is insufficient to defend market share. The structural shift toward gold and vehicles is clear. Lenders should maintain housing exposure for its scale and stability, but incremental capital should flow to higher-growth segments.

inferences: Housing grew 10.9% YoY in May 2026, the weakest reading in this 9-period window.
The 0.5-point deceleration from Apr 2026 breaks the brief re-acceleration that peaked at 11.5% in Mar 2026.
Housing demand is stable but insufficient to defend market share against gold and vehicles.

insights.title: Other PL 24.9% share — lowest in 9 periods
insights.body: Other Personal Loans account for 24.9% of personal loans in May 2026, the lowest level in nine periods. Share held flat at 24.9% from Apr 2026 to May 2026, after declining from 25.8% in Jan 2025.
insights.implication: Other PL is losing share despite accelerating growth — a sign that gold and vehicles are the true momentum plays. This category remains large (24.9% share) and is growing faster than housing, but it is no longer the incremental growth leader. Lenders should monitor for further share erosion.

inferences: Other PL's share fell from 25.8% in Jan 2025 to 24.9% in May 2026 — a 0.9pp structural decline.
The share has stabilized at 24.9% for two consecutive periods, but remains at the lower end of the range.
Other PL is losing share despite accelerating growth — a sign that gold and vehicles are the true momentum plays.

insights.title: Other PL 12.5% YoY — growth holding near highs
insights.body: Other Personal Loans grew 12.5% YoY in May 2026, down slightly from 12.7% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated by 0.2 points period-on-period (12.7% → 12.5%), holding near the upper end of the 9-period range (7.5% to 12.8%).
insights.implication: Other PL has inflected sharply and is now growing faster than housing. This category — which includes personal loans not classified elsewhere — is regaining momentum. Lenders should watch for sustained acceleration, but gold and vehicles remain the higher-growth opportunities.

inferences: Other PL grew 12.5% YoY in May 2026, near the 9-period high of 12.8%.
This category has inflected sharply from 7.5% in Mar 2025 to 12.5% in May 2026 — a 5.0-point acceleration.
Other PL is now growing faster than housing (10.9%) but slower than gold (105.5%) and vehicles.

insights.title: Advances against shares 0.15% — immaterial to mix
insights.body: Advances against shares and bonds account for 0.15% of personal loans in May 2026. Share held flat at 0.1% to 0.2% across the 9-period window, with no meaningful movement.
insights.implication: This category is immaterial. Lenders can ignore it for portfolio allocation purposes.

inferences: Advances against shares account for 0.15% of personal loans, unchanged from Apr 2026.
This category has remained immaterial across the entire 9-period window.
The scale is too small to influence portfolio allocation decisions.

insights.title: Housing is the biggest slice of personal loans at 48.0%
insights.body: Housing holds the largest share of personal loans at 48.0%, Other Personal Loans 24.9%; Advances to Individuals against share, bonds, etc. is the smallest at 0.1%. These are size shares, not growth rates. Top three hold 83.5% of personal loans, spread 47.9 percentage points.
insights.implication: Composition, not momentum: Housing carries the most weight of personal loans. Concentration means the big blocks drive the category — watch them first.

inferences: Housing is the largest block at 48.0% of personal loans.
Advances to Individuals against share, bonds, etc. is the smallest at 0.1% — a 47.9 percentage points spread across 9 sub-categories.
The top three hold 83.5% of personal loans — concentrated mix.

insights.title: Vehicle loans 10.7% share — stable in upper range
insights.body: Vehicle loans account for 10.7% of personal loans in May 2026, unchanged from Apr 2026. Share has held steady in the 10.4%–10.7% range across nine periods, with no meaningful drift.
insights.implication: Vehicles are growing fast but in line with the overall personal loan pool — they are not gaining share but are a reliable growth contributor. This is a stable, high-growth segment worth maintaining or expanding exposure.

inferences: Vehicle loans' share has held at 10.7% for two consecutive periods, in the upper half of the 9-period range.
Vehicles are growing fast but in line with the overall personal loan pool — they are not gaining share but are a reliable growth contributor.
This is a stable, high-growth segment worth maintaining or expanding exposure.

insights.title: Vehicle loans 17.3% YoY — growth moderating from 18.6% peak
insights.body: Vehicle loans grew 17.33% YoY in May 2026, down from 17.97% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 0.64 points period-on-period, stepping down from the Mar 2026 peak of 18.6% → 18.0% → 17.3%.
insights.implication: Vehicle lending remains robust at 17.3%, but the momentum is cooling. The three-period slowdown from 18.6% signals the inflection may have peaked. Lenders should continue to prioritize vehicle finance — it is still the second-fastest major category after gold — but temper expectations of further acceleration. Watch the next period: if growth holds above 17%, the plateau is healthy; if it drops below 16%, the cycle may be turning.

inferences: Vehicle loans grew 17.33% YoY in May 2026, the third consecutive deceleration from the 18.6% peak in Mar 2026.
The rate has fallen 1.3 points in two periods (18.6% → 17.3%), though it remains near the upper quartile (p75=17.7%) of the nine-period range.
The trajectory suggests the sharp inflection that began in Dec 2025 (16.5%) may be plateauing rather than accelerating further.
If the deceleration continues, vehicle loans will converge toward the mid-teens range, still strong but no longer the second-fastest major category.

insights.title: Loans against gold jewellery growing fastest at 105.5%; Consumer Durables slowest at -2.6%
insights.body: Loans against gold jewellery leads YoY growth at 105.5%, Vehicle Loans at 17.3%; Consumer Durables is slowest at -2.6%. 8 of 9 categories growing, spread 108.1 percentage points.
insights.implication: Lenders can lean into Loans against gold jewellery (105.5%) and monitor Consumer Durables (-2.6%). Broad participation supports diversified deployment.

inferences: Loans against gold jewellery is the standout at 105.5% YoY.
Consumer Durables is the weakest at -2.6% — a spread of 108.1 percentage points across 9 categories.
8 of 9 categories are growing — broad-based momentum.

gaps.title: Consumer Durables negative for 9 straight periods — record contraction
gaps.body: Consumer Durables have posted negative YoY growth for 9 consecutive periods, the longest contraction streak on record. The negative streak extended from 8 periods in Apr 2026 to 9 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.
gaps.implication: Consumer durable financing is in terminal decline — lenders have effectively exited this segment. Expect continued contraction unless consumption rebounds sharply, which is not visible in the data. No capital allocation warranted.

inferences: Consumer Durables have contracted for 9 consecutive periods, the longest negative run in this dataset.
The streak began in Jan 2025 and has persisted through May 2026 without a single positive reading.
This confirms a structural exit by lenders from consumer durable financing.

gaps.title: Consumer Durables at -2.64% YoY — decline moderating
gaps.body: Consumer Durables outstanding contracted 2.64% YoY in May 2026, an improvement from the -3.38% decline in Apr 2026. The contraction moderated -3.38% → -2.64%, the smallest decline since Jan 2025's -2.4%.
gaps.implication: The worst of the contraction appears to be behind us, but the segment remains in decline at -2.6% YoY. Recovery is not yet visible — lenders should continue to de-prioritise this category until positive growth returns.

inferences: Consumer Durables contracted 2.64% YoY in May 2026, the smallest decline since Jan 2025.
The worst of the contraction was Feb 2026's -9.8%; the rate has improved 7.2 points since then.
The moderation suggests the segment is stabilising, though still in decline.

gaps.title: Housing share 48.0% — lowest level in 9 periods
gaps.body: Housing loans account for 48.0% of personal loans in May 2026, the lowest level in nine periods. Share declined from 48.2% in Apr 2026 to 48.0% in May 2026, extending the year-long slide from 50.4% in Jan 2025.
gaps.implication: Housing is losing ground in a growing personal loan pool. The 10.9% growth rate is healthy in isolation, but it is being outrun by gold (105.5%), vehicles, and other PL. Lenders overweight in housing are missing the fastest-growing pockets — portfolio rebalancing toward gold and vehicles is warranted.

inferences: Housing's share fell from 50.4% in Jan 2025 to 48.0% in May 2026 — a 2.4pp structural decline.
This is the lowest reading in the dataset, and the slide has been uninterrupted for 16 months.
Housing is losing ground in a growing personal loan pool, outrun by gold, vehicles, and other PL.

gaps.title: Advances against shares 8.3% YoY — reversal from contraction
gaps.body: Advances against shares grew 8.3% YoY in May 2026, reversing from -3.6% in Apr 2026. Growth surged by 11.9 points period-on-period (-3.6% → 8.3%), the sharpest single-period reversal in this window.
gaps.implication: This category has reversed from contraction to growth, but at 0.15% share, it is immaterial to portfolio strategy. The sharp swing may signal tighter or looser risk appetite for leveraged equity lending, but the scale is too small to act on.

inferences: Advances against shares grew 8.3% YoY in May 2026, up from -3.6% in Apr 2026 — an 11.9-point reversal.
This is the sharpest single-period swing in the series, but the category accounts for only 0.15% of personal loans.
The reversal may signal renewed risk appetite for leveraged equity lending, but the scale is too small to matter.

insights.title: PSL Agriculture 13.8% YoY — 3.5pp deceleration from 17.3%
insights.body: PSL Agriculture credit grew 13.84% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 17.33% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 3.49pp from 17.3% → 13.8%, breaking a six-period acceleration from 11.0% in Mar 2025.
insights.implication: Agriculture lending remains healthy at 13.8%, but the sharp deceleration from 17.3% signals the peak may have passed. Banks should monitor whether this is a seasonal correction or the start of a structural slowdown — hold current exposure but pause aggressive expansion until the trend stabilises.

inferences: Agriculture credit grew 13.84% YoY in May 2026, down from 17.33% in Apr 2026 — a 3.49pp deceleration.
This breaks a six-period climb from 11.0% in Mar 2025 to 17.3% in Apr 2026, the first meaningful pullback since the uptrend began.
The deceleration suggests the kharif-driven surge may be moderating, though growth remains above the historical median of 11.6%.

insights.title: PSL Education Loans 22.6% YoY — stable near upper range
insights.body: PSL Educational Loans grew 22.59% year-on-year in May 2026, down marginally from 23.27% in Apr 2026. Growth eased 0.68pp from 23.3% → 22.6%, holding in the 22–24% band for six straight periods since Dec 2025.
insights.implication: Education lending is locked in at 22–24% growth — a structural opportunity. Banks should continue scaling origination capacity and expect sustained demand through the academic cycle. The 0.68pp dip is noise within a stable high-growth regime.

inferences: Education loans grew 22.59% YoY in May 2026, down 0.68pp from 23.27% in Apr 2026.
Growth has held in the 22–24% range for six consecutive periods, with no reading below 22.6% since the breakout from near-zero growth in Mar 2025.
The stability at high growth confirms this is a structural re-rating, not a cyclical spike.

insights.title: PSL Housing 39.0% YoY — holding near 40% for six periods
insights.body: PSL Housing credit grew 38.98% year-on-year in May 2026, down marginally from 39.94% in Apr 2026. Growth eased 0.96pp from 39.9% → 39.0%, holding in the 37–40% band for six straight periods since Dec 2025.
insights.implication: Housing is the anchor of PSL growth at 39.0% — demand is structural, not cyclical. Banks should maximise exposure to this segment and expect sustained deployment through FY27. The 0.96pp dip is noise within a stable high-growth regime.

inferences: Housing credit grew 38.98% YoY in May 2026, down 0.96pp from 39.94% in Apr 2026.
Growth has held in the 37–40% range for six consecutive periods, with no reading below 36.9% since the breakout from negative growth in Mar 2025.
The stability at near-40% growth confirms this is the structural engine of PSL expansion.

insights.title: PSL Medium Enterprises 17.8% YoY — reversing deceleration trend
insights.body: PSL Medium Enterprises credit grew 17.84% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 17.24% in Apr 2026. Growth accelerated 0.59pp from 17.2% → 17.8%, the first uptick after a five-period deceleration from 22.6% in Mar 2025.
insights.implication: Medium enterprises showed a 0.59pp uptick to 17.8%, but it is too early to call a trend reversal — growth remains below the historical p25. Banks should monitor whether this stabilises above 18% before scaling exposure, as the segment has lost share to Micro & Small throughout the deceleration.

inferences: Medium enterprises credit grew 17.84% YoY in May 2026, up 0.59pp from 17.24% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration after a five-period slide from 22.6% in Mar 2025 → 17.2% in Apr 2026.
Growth remains below the historical p25 of 17.9%, signaling this segment is still in the lower range despite the uptick.

insights.title: PSL MSME 29.4% in FY26 vs 13.4% in FY25 — 15.9pp step-up
insights.body: PSL MSME credit grew 13.4% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 29.4% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 15.9-point acceleration. Full-year growth more than doubled from 13.4% in FY25 to 29.4% in FY26, marking the largest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
insights.implication: MSME's 15.9pp full-year acceleration from FY25 to FY26 is the structural growth story in PSL. Banks should expect this momentum to anchor FY27 performance — scale origination capacity and monitor credit quality closely as volumes compound at near-30% growth.

inferences: MSME credit grew 13.4% in FY25 and 29.4% in FY26 — a 15.9-point step-up, the largest in this dataset.
This full-year acceleration reflects the structural breakout in Micro & Small lending, which surged from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to 29.4% in Mar 2026.
The 15.9pp gap confirms MSME is the primary driver of PSL expansion, anchoring FY26 performance.

insights.title: PSL Micro & Small 23.7% YoY — 4.8pp deceleration from 28.5%
insights.body: PSL Micro & Small enterprises credit grew 23.69% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 28.53% in Apr 2026. Growth decelerated 4.85pp from 28.5% → 23.7%, the sharpest single-period drop since the breakout from 13.4% in Mar 2025.
insights.implication: Micro & Small decelerated sharply from 28.5% to 23.7%, but growth remains robust at 23.7% — well above the pre-breakout 13.4%. Banks should monitor whether this stabilises above 20% or decelerates further. The structural trend is intact, but the pace is slowing from the 29.4% peak in Mar 2026.

inferences: Micro & Small credit grew 23.69% YoY in May 2026, down 4.85pp from 28.53% in Apr 2026.
This is the sharpest deceleration since the surge from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to 24.8% in Jan 2026, though growth remains near the historical median of 28.5%.
The 4.85pp drop suggests the peak growth phase may be moderating, but the structural trend remains intact above 20%.

insights.title: PSL Renewable Energy 12 straight periods of YoY growth — record streak
insights.body: PSL Renewable Energy has posted positive year-on-year growth for 12 consecutive periods, the longest streak in this dataset. The streak extended from 11 periods in Apr 2026 to 12 in May 2026, with no interruption since the series began in Jan 2025.
insights.implication: Renewable Energy's 12-period positive streak is the longest on record — this is a structurally entrenched opportunity. Banks should expect sustained demand through FY27 and beyond, with policy support and ESG mandates anchoring growth. Scale exposure with confidence.

inferences: Renewable Energy has grown YoY for 12 consecutive periods, extending from 11 in Apr 2026 to 12 in May 2026.
This is the longest positive streak in the dataset, with no interruption since the series began.
The unbroken streak confirms Renewable Energy lending is structurally entrenched in positive territory, supported by policy and ESG tailwinds.

insights.title: PSL Renewable Energy 17.5% YoY — accelerating from 15.6%
insights.body: PSL Renewable Energy grew 17.46% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 15.64% in Apr 2026. Growth accelerated 1.82pp from 15.6% → 17.5%, the first uptick after a six-period deceleration from 78.3% in Mar 2025.
insights.implication: Renewable Energy accelerated from 15.6% to 17.5%, the first uptick after six periods of deceleration. This may signal stabilisation, but growth remains below the p25 of 21.7%. Banks should monitor whether this holds above 17% for two more periods before scaling exposure — the 12-period positive streak is intact, but the pace is still recovering.

inferences: Renewable Energy credit grew 17.46% YoY in May 2026, up 1.82pp from 15.64% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration after a six-period slide from 78.3% in Mar 2025 → 15.6% in Apr 2026.
Growth remains below the historical p25 of 21.7%, signaling the segment is still in the lower range despite the uptick.

insights.title: PSL Social Infrastructure 70.7% YoY — surging 35.4pp from 35.3%
insights.body: PSL Social Infrastructure grew 70.71% year-on-year in May 2026, surging from 35.27% in Apr 2026. Growth accelerated 35.44pp from 35.3% → 70.7%, the highest reading in the nine-period series and the largest single-period jump on record.
insights.implication: Social Infrastructure surged 35.4pp to 70.7%, the highest on record, but the extreme volatility — from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 70.7% in May 2026 — makes this segment unpredictable. Banks should treat this as a tactical opportunity, not a structural trend, and avoid scaling exposure until the trajectory stabilises for at least three consecutive periods.

inferences: Social Infrastructure credit grew 70.71% YoY in May 2026, up 35.44pp from 35.27% in Apr 2026.
This is the highest reading in the series and the largest single-period jump, after swinging from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 12.9% in Mar 2026 to 35.3% in Apr 2026.
The 35.44pp surge confirms extreme volatility — the segment has swung from negative to 70.7% in just three periods.

insights.title: PSL Weaker Sections 9.1% YoY — weakest reading on record
insights.body: PSL Weaker Sections (including PSLC SF/MF) grew 9.06% year-on-year in May 2026, the weakest reading in the nine-period series. Growth decelerated 1.61pp from 10.7% → 9.1%, extending a six-period slide from 15.3% in Mar 2025 and now below the historical p25 of 10.9%.
insights.implication: Weaker Sections at 9.1% is the weakest on record, losing share to Micro & Small and Housing. This reflects either demand weakness or strategic reallocation by banks toward higher-growth segments. Banks should assess credit quality in this segment — if demand is structurally weak, policy interventions may be needed to revive lending.

inferences: Weaker Sections credit grew 9.06% YoY in May 2026, down 1.61pp from 10.67% in Apr 2026.
This is the weakest reading in the series, below the historical p25 of 10.9%, after a six-period deceleration from 15.3% in Mar 2025.
The segment is losing share to Micro & Small (23.7%) and Housing (39.0%), which are growing 2.6× and 4.3× faster.

gaps.title: PSL Export Credit -9.7% YoY — contraction easing from -12.0%
gaps.body: PSL Export Credit contracted 9.66% year-on-year in May 2026, improving from -12.02% in Apr 2026. The contraction narrowed 2.36pp from -12.0% → -9.7%, resuming the easing trend after Apr's reversal from -8.4% in Mar 2026.
gaps.implication: Export credit is stabilising but not yet recovering — the move from -12.0% to -9.7% is progress, but growth remains negative. Banks should stay cautious on export lending until the contraction turns positive for at least two consecutive periods.

inferences: Export credit contracted 9.66% YoY in May 2026, improving from -12.02% in Apr 2026 — a 2.36pp narrowing.
This resumes the easing trend from -17.2% in Jan 2026 → -8.8% in Mar 2026, after Apr's reversal to -12.0%.
The contraction remains near the historical median of -13.9%, signaling persistent weakness in trade finance.

gaps.title: PSL Others -11.2% YoY — contraction easing from -12.5%
gaps.body: PSL Others contracted 11.20% year-on-year in May 2026, improving from -12.45% in Apr 2026. The contraction narrowed 1.25pp from -12.5% → -11.2%, resuming the easing trend after Apr's reversal from -9.6% in Mar 2026.
gaps.implication: PSL Others is stabilising — the move from -12.5% to -11.2% is the weakest contraction on record, above the p75 of -11.8%. Banks should watch whether this turns positive in the next 1–2 periods, signaling a structural recovery in miscellaneous PSL categories.

inferences: PSL Others contracted 11.20% YoY in May 2026, improving from -12.45% in Apr 2026 — a 1.25pp narrowing.
